name: Team Tactics
summary: Team Tactics is a fail-closed TDD harness for Claude Code. Install with npx tics into any Claude Code project to turn AI edits into a disciplined pairing loop. The gate: every edit is checked by phase and layer — out-of-scope files are blocked, run-suite emits objective RED/GREEN signals, and the orchestrator never gets to declare the job done on a red bar. Role agents: test-writer adds exactly one failing characterization test; implementer writes the minimal code to pass it; tdd-critic audits behavior vs implementation every few cycles. Tic bus: agents coordinate through .claude/state/tics.jsonl (append-only), recording every delegate, handoff, signal, block, and verdict. Parallel pairs auto-claim files — no collisions. tics log shows the thread; tics gate checks release readiness. A hand-rolled MCP server — JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio, written from scratch with no SDK and zero dependencies — exposes the tic bus to any MCP-capable client, so agents in Cursor or other tools can read bus state and emit via tic_emit without being Claude Code hooks; signal, block, and commit stay hook-only and unforgeable. Composable layers: @ttics/tics is the protocol alone, @ttics/tdd is the pairing gate, team-tactics composes both with full-team installed by default. A Stop-hook nudge surfaces solo-drift as an advisory accountability check when a single agent accumulates substantial unsupervised changes. Adopt incrementally — private full-team repo available on request.
why_it_matters: The open, composable TDD kit behind all agentic work on this site. Fail-closed hooks (red = tests, green = source, can't finish on red), role agents (test-writer, implementer, tdd-critic), and a live tic bus so every handoff is recorded and parallel pairs never collide. The same harness that shipped the contact pipeline, chat backend (Phases 1-4), and every invariant on this site.

name: AI assistant & chatbot
summary: Grounded AI assistant — SSE-streaming text chat, Gemini Live browser-direct voice, automatic model fallback, and durable per-turn telemetry. The AI on this site runs on FastAPI + direct google-genai SDK on ECS Express Mode. POST /api/chat with stream: true returns text/event-stream — event: token per chunk, event: done on completion, event: error on failure. The first token typically lands in under a second. Grounded answers: a knowledge pack built from resume, project, and bio sources is injected into every turn. Automatic model fallback: on primary rate-limit, the routing chain transparently retries the secondary model — but only if no chunk has flushed yet; once a token reaches the client the chain commits. Voice (Gemini Live): POST /api/live/session mints an ephemeral token; the browser opens Google's Live API directly — no server relay, works from any HTTPS host. Durable per-turn telemetry: every turn (success, error, or timeout) writes a DynamoDB row before the response returns, capturing stream, firstTokenLatencyMs, fallbackUsed, status, errorCode, toolCalls, and retrieval snapshot. Admin observability: private dashboard with streamed-session cards, 30-day sparkline, errors-by-code table, and a clickable recent-failures list. What it demonstrates: the engineering reality of production AI — streaming UX, fallback routing, durable failure capture, and observability are where the work actually lives.
why_it_matters: Production AI reality: the model is rarely the hard part. Streaming UX, fallback routing, durable failure capture, and observability are where the work lives. Demonstrates Marwan's habits applied to a personal site where every choice is visible.

name: Monday Rover (Raspberry Pi)
summary: Real-time computer vision on constrained hardware — a Raspberry Pi rover. Monday Rover (Raspberry Pi) — a personal exploration of real-time computer vision on constrained hardware. A Raspberry Pi rover with motor control, camera capture, and an on-device vision loop. Built with version control, clear module boundaries, and a reproducible setup. What it demonstrates. Latency budgeting and reliable behavior under motion on hardware that can't paper over inefficiency. Edge-device thinking with production habits attached. Scope: exploration, not product — single-purpose, not hardened beyond a controlled environment.
why_it_matters: Demonstrates Marwan's practical approach to delivering reliable, maintainable systems.

name: Generative Video Platform
summary: End-to-end generative AI for video — product UX, orchestration, and the slow/expensive/variable paths. Generative Video Platform — an end-to-end exploration of generative AI applied to video workflows. A working prototype with a prompt UX, an orchestration layer, and the plumbing to handle paths that are slow, expensive, and high-variance — which is most of generative AI in production. What it demonstrates. The engineering reality of production AI. The model is rarely the hard part. Latency, cost, output variability, and product UX are where the work actually lives. Scope: learning prototype — not load-tested, not cost-optimized for production.
why_it_matters: Demonstrates how Marwan combines product UX with clear service boundaries for AI-enabled workflows.

name: Apptio (an IBM company)
summary: Migrated financial pipelines to a 10× faster architecture with zero correctness drift. Apptio (an IBM company) — Senior Software Engineer · 2023–2025. Migrated Apptio's financial-data pipelines from Java/Kubernetes to EMR Spark with Scala — and proved every byte still added up. The hard part. Financial datasets have zero tolerance for drift. A 10× faster migration with even small discrepancies would be worse than the original. A big-bang cutover would have made any discrepancy impossible to isolate. What I did. Migrated the heavy workloads to EMR Spark with Scala for throughput on large datasets. Built a parallel reconciliation pipeline: legacy and new ran side-by-side on the same inputs, and the pipeline flagged any difference in real time. That turned a risky migration into an incremental one — each workload cut over independently, verified against the system it replaced, then retired. Automated operations with Python, Lambda, and Step Functions; managed AWS infrastructure with Terraform. Outcome. Roughly 10× faster processing. Zero correctness drift on financial datasets. Service availability held throughout. What it demonstrates. On correctness-critical systems, the migration is the easy half. The proof of correctness is the half that matters. Build the reconciliation harness first.
why_it_matters: Shows Marwan's approach to platform modernization in data-intensive SaaS with strong operability guardrails.

name: JumpCloud
summary: M365 integration on a hybrid Postgres/Mongo + GraphQL layer for 50,000+ tenants. JumpCloud — Senior Software Engineer · 2022–2023. Directed end-to-end technical design for a Microsoft M365 integration on JumpCloud's cloud directory platform — 50,000+ tenants in production. The hard part. The data layer had to do two things that pull opposite directions: serve fast relational queries over the core identity graph, and absorb high write volume of per-tenant custom attributes with flexible, variable shapes. A pure relational store strained under writes. A pure document store gave up the relational performance the identity graph depended on. What I did. Architected a hybrid data layer — PostgreSQL for the identity graph (relational, consistent, fast), MongoDB for per-tenant attributes (variable, write-heavy). Designed the multi-tenant GraphQL schema and resolvers. Built the integration end to end with Docker, Go, gRPC, and Node.js. Outcome. Shipped to 50,000+ production tenants. Both requirements satisfied — each routed to the store suited to it. What it demonstrates. When the problem genuinely has two shapes, don't force one. A hybrid means more moving parts; it also means you stop fighting the data.
why_it_matters: Reflects Marwan's work in identity-critical systems where reliability and safe rollout matter.

name: Hewlett Packard (HP)
summary: Scaled HP Instant Ink thousands → millions via DDD-driven cloud migration. Hewlett Packard (HP) — Specialist Software Engineer · 2018–2021. Scaled HP's Instant Ink subscription service from thousands of subscribers to millions, globally, in months. The hard part. Instant Ink ran on a Ruby on Rails monolith — fine at its original scale, structurally unfit for orders-of-magnitude growth. The work was not a tuning exercise; it was a structural transformation that had to happen on a live, revenue-generating service, without disrupting customers, across four geographically distributed teams. What I did. Planned and led the transformation. Applied Domain-Driven Design to decompose the Rails monolith into cloud-native microservices and data pipelines on AWS — Lambda, API Gateway, SNS, SQS, DynamoDB, Redshift, CloudFormation. Drove TDD and agile practices across the four teams, working closely with DevOps, QA, and Product Management. Outcome. Thousands to millions of subscribers worldwide. Months, not years. Reliability and stability held throughout — customers experienced growth, not disruption. What it demonstrates. Domain-Driven Design doubles as architecture and as an organizational tool. The service boundaries it drew were the same boundaries four distributed teams could work along — which is why the migration could move quickly without the teams colliding.
why_it_matters: Highlights experience evolving subscription systems to larger scale while protecting reliability.

name: AT&T
summary: TQL — a SQL-like query language for ticket search and operational analytics. AT&T — Full Stack Engineer · 2017–2018. Built TQL: a SQL-like query language for ticket search and operational analytics on a service-oriented single-page application. The hard part. Support and ops teams needed fast, repeatable search and analytics over ticket data. Ad-hoc filters and one-off scripts didn't scale with ticket volume — or with analysts who needed predictable query semantics and bounded execution. What I did. Designed and built TQL — grammar, validation, and a test-covered execution path. Built front-end and back-end components, designed RESTful APIs, and developed async JavaScript modules. Mentored new developers and raised code-quality standards across the team. Outcome. Production SOA application with a usable query surface for real support workflows. What it demonstrates. Expressiveness and safety move together, not against each other. A query language has to be useful to analysts and predictable for operators in the same design.
why_it_matters: Shows foundational backend work where query semantics and operational usefulness were both central.
